While on the expedition to the South Pole, Sir Ernest Shackleton, who was a British explorer, left a few men behind on Elephant Island while he went elsewhere. And he promised that he would return to get them. Later, when he tried to go back, huge icebergs blocked his way to the island. He was able to get there. But suddenly, as if by a miracle one day, the icebergs moved out of the way and he got a path into the island, went in, hurriedly got the men on board the ship and left. And just as they got out of the passage to the island, the icebergs Berks came back together again. So it was a pretty narrow escape. Contemplating this, he said to his men, it was fortunate. You were all packed and ready to go when I got there. And they said, well, we never gave up hope. Whenever the sea began to clear vice, we had everything packed and ready to go. They were ready and watching for him to come back. Now, in the New Testament, Roman soldiers would set watches to guard the camp at night. So the New Testament seems to have adopted the Roman army's watch times of which there were four. The first watch was 6 p.m. to 9 p.m. The second watch was 9 p.m. The second watch was 9 p.m. to 12 a.m. The third watch was 12 a.m. to 3 a.m. And the fourth watch was 3 a.m. 6 a.m. Thus you find when Jesus walks on the water and goes out to the ship, he does it in the fourth watch of the night, according to Matthew 1425. He does it between 3 a.m. and 6 a.m. That's pretty late, isn't it? But the point here is whether Christ's, arrives early or whether he comes late.
